title: THE FOUR OLDS — sc03 the barn and the Tuesday net

panel.gen: Interior of a Nebraska barn at night lit by a single hanging work light: a homebuilt spacecraft simulator of plywood and salvaged instrument panels with a real crew couch bolted to a welded frame, and along the whole back wall, floor-to-roof shelves packed with hundreds of identical spiral notebooks, an old man in the couch wearing a headset, a glowing ham radio on the bench at his elbow.

lines.text: [calm] The Dawes barn. A work light on a beam. Filling half the barn: a homebuilt simulator — plywood, angle iron, salvaged instrument panels, a real crew couch bolted to a welded frame. Every switch is real. Every label is hand-lettered. Along the back wall, floor to roof: spiral notebooks, hundreds of them, spines out, each spine dated in the same hand. The first shelf starts in nineteen seventy-two. Cricket drops into the couch and brings the panel up. An amber bus light steadies. He keys the ham rig.
[steady, routine] Net's open. Tuesday, nineteen-oh-two local.
[warm drawl] There he is.
[brisk, no preamble] Bus B first. What's it read.
[matter-of-fact] Twenty-eight point one.

[grudging] Huh. Okay. So it held. I still don't like the way it sagged last week. When's the last time you pulled that relay — don't answer, I have it here. Eighty-four. You put that relay in in eighty-four.
[dry, needling] Is he doing the relay again?
[defensive, factual] It's a forty-year-old relay, is all I'm saying. I'm allowed to say it.
[firm, drill-floor courtesy] It is nineteen-oh-three, gentlemen. Commander, run your checklist.
[crisp] Guidance internal. Tanks at sixty-two percent, simulated. Descent program loaded.
The barn door rolls open a foot. Peg, seventy-seven, in a house coat, sets a thermos on the workbench.
[dry, unimpressed] Tell Elmore his wife wants him off by ten.
Cricket lifts two fingers off the throttle without looking back. Peg pulls the door to behind her.
[perking up] Was that Peg?
[deadpan] Coffee's here.
[starting in] Tell her I said—
[cutting him off] It's an open checklist, people.
[low, tightening] Cricket flies. Hands easy on the controls. The panel needles track. Then an ammeter needle drops and wanders. A low buzz under the hum.
[flat, alert] Bus B again. Needle's walking.
[sharp, diagnostic] That buzz. Steady or does it stutter?
Stutters.
[certain] Inverter case. Kick it, low left corner.
[beat] Cricket kicks the case, low left corner. The needle climbs home. The buzz quits. Nobody says anything about it. The checklist goes on.
[quiet focus] Three hundred feet. Drifting left. Correcting.
[hushed] Silence on the net. Only the hum and the switch clicks. His hands make small moves. The rate needle eases down. Down. A panel lamp lights: CONTACT.
[even, a breath of satisfaction] Contact light. Engine stop.
[needling, affectionate] Hair late on the throttle there at the end.
[final] The man is on the ground, Colonel.
[mock innocence] I'm just saying what I saw.
[closing the book] Net closed. Same time Tuesday.
[plain] God willing.

[quiet] Cricket keys the mic twice — two clicks. Two clicks come back. Then two more. Then two more. He powers the rig down, takes a fresh page, and writes in block letters: T plus two thousand eight hundred six. Bus B corrected. Contact, nineteen forty-one. Crew present by radio. [beat] He shelves the notebook with the others, runs his thumb once down the dated spines on his way to the light — nineteen seventy-four, nineteen eighty-eight, two thousand three — and pulls the cord.

panel.gen: A Nebraska farmyard at night, an old man padlocking the rolling door of a big dark barn, warm porch light glowing from the white farmhouse across the yard, night sky above, brush-ink heavy blacks.

lines.text: [slow] He rolls the door shut and snaps a padlock through the hasp. Tests it with one pull. [beat] Beyond the barn, the farmhouse porch light. He crosses to the house, thermos under his arm.
